title: Falling sick often: building resilience
excerpt: If you seem to catch every cold going around, Ayurveda has a two-thousand-year-old explanation: your ojas, the body's finest vital essence, needs rebuilding. Here is how the classical texts say to do it.
at_a_glance: Vyadhikshamatva is Ayurveda's term for the body's inherent resistance to disease, and it can be strengthened.
Ojas, the finest product of good digestion and rest, is the deep reserve that keeps infections from taking hold.
Consistent mealtimes, sleep before midnight, moderate exercise, and warming foods are the classical foundation for rebuilding it.
agni_desc: The goal is a steady, balanced fire (sama). Too low leaves food undigested; too sharp burns through it. An irregular fire (vishama) swings between the two.

body.heading: why you keep falling sick
paragraphs: You wash your hands. You avoid sharing food. You even wear a jacket when the weather turns. Yet somehow you are the one who goes down with every seasonal bug, every throat infection, every bout of fatigue that sweeps the office. If this sounds familiar, Ayurveda would say the question is not what germ found you: it is why your body could not resist it.

The classical answer rests on a concept called vyadhikshamatva, which translates roughly as the capacity to withstand disease. Caraka, writing in the Sutrasthana of the Caraka Samhita, describes this inherent resistance as what separates those who fall ill from those who do not when they face the same external trigger (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). The text treats vyadhikshamatva as something that can be cultivated, not merely inherited.
body.pullQuote: however wholesome a food is, it only produces ojas if agni is working well enough to convert it

body.heading: ojas: the deep reserve behind immunity
paragraphs: Underneath vyadhikshamatva sits a subtler idea: ojas. The word means vigour or lustre. The Caraka Samhita describes ojas as the finest product of digestion and tissue transformation, a kind of concentrated vitality that accumulates when each of the seven body tissues (dhatus) has been nourished properly in sequence (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). Sushruta Samhita similarly places ojas at the heart of life force, noting that its depletion leads to fear, weakness and susceptibility to illness (Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana).

What depletes ojas? The texts list causes that feel very modern: irregular sleep, eating at odd hours, too much dry or processed food, chronic worry, overwork, and suppression of natural urges. Ashtanga Hridaya, the seventh-century synthesis by Vagbhata, adds excessive fasting and emotional grief to the list (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). In other words, the life most people lead in a city is, from an Ayurvedic standpoint, quietly draining the very reserve that keeps infections at bay.

Low ojas does not produce one specific disease. Instead it produces a pattern: you recover slowly, you tire easily, your digestion feels unreliable, your skin looks dull, and you are the first person in any room to catch whatever is going around. The Caraka Samhita describes this diminished state as the ground in which illness takes root (Caraka Samhita, Nidanasthana).

body.heading: building resilience: food, routine, rest
paragraphs: The classical texts are consistent on one point: ojas is rebuilt through nourishment and rest, not through a single herb or remedy. The most practical place to start is agni, the digestive fire. Caraka emphasises that however wholesome a food is, it only produces ojas if agni is working well enough to convert it (Caraka Samhita, Chikitsasthana). This means eating at consistent times, choosing warm cooked food over cold and raw, and avoiding heavy meals late at night.

Sleep deserves equal attention. Ashtanga Hridaya treats sleep as one of the three pillars of life alongside food and brahmacharya (right conduct), and specifies that sleeping before midnight and waking before sunrise protects both ojas and mental clarity (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). Chronic late nights are, by this logic, one of the fastest routes to repeated illness.

Gentle, consistent exercise, called vyayama, is recommended in the Caraka Samhita up to half one's capacity: enough to bring a light sweat to the forehead and armpits, but not so much that you are breathless or exhausted (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). This kind of moderate movement improves circulation to the tissues and supports the sequential transformation that builds ojas. Intense daily exercise without adequate recovery, by contrast, strains the tissues rather than nourishing them.

Among foods the classical tradition singles out warm milk, ghee, sesame, honey (not heated), dates, and fresh seasonal grains as ojas-building. Cumin, coriander, fennel and ginger as everyday kitchen spices support digestion so that whatever you eat is actually absorbed. Bhavaprakasha, which catalogues medicinal plants in detail, describes ashwagandha and shatavari as rasayanas: plants that rebuild tissue and strengthen resistance when taken consistently over weeks (Bhavaprakasha, Haritakyadi Varga). These are not quick fixes; the texts treat rasayana as a seasonal or monthly practice, not a one-dose cure.

body.heading: when to see a doctor
paragraphs: The habits above are general wellness guidance drawn from classical texts. They are not a diagnosis, and they are not a substitute for medical care. If you are falling sick very frequently, if infections are severe, prolonged, or unusual, if you have unexplained weight loss, persistent fever, swollen lymph nodes, or extreme fatigue that does not improve with rest, please consult a qualified doctor or a registered vaidya. These can be signs of an underlying condition, including immune disorders, diabetes, anaemia, or thyroid imbalance, that needs proper investigation. Ayurvedic guidance works best alongside, not instead of, a proper medical evaluation when symptoms are significant.

try_this_today.title: Eat at the same time each day
try_this_today.description: Pick a regular time for each meal and stick to it for two weeks. Caraka Samhita notes that regular mealtimes train agni, the digestive fire, to work at its strongest when food arrives.

try_this_today.title: Add a small spoon of ghee to your dal or rice
try_this_today.description: Ghee is listed repeatedly in the classical texts as an ojas-building food. A teaspoon with a warm cooked meal also helps fat-soluble vitamins absorb.

try_this_today.title: Wind down by 10 pm
try_this_today.description: Ashtanga Hridaya treats sleep before midnight as protective of ojas. Even 30 minutes earlier than your usual bedtime is a meaningful start.

try_this_today.title: Use cumin, coriander and fennel in cooking
try_this_today.description: These three together are a classic Ayurvedic digestive combination. Add them to a tempering (tadka) or brew them lightly as a post-meal tea to support absorption.

try_this_today.title: Walk after lunch
try_this_today.description: A gentle 10-15 minute walk after the midday meal, described in the Caraka Samhita as shatapavali, is said to aid digestion without overtaxing the system.

try_this_today.title: Consider a short rasayana course in autumn or spring
try_this_today.description: Seasonal transitions are when resistance typically dips. Consult a vaidya about a four-to-six-week course of ashwagandha or shatavari, the rasayana herbs the Bhavaprakasha recommends for rebuilding tissue strength.

ingredients.name: Ginger
ingredients.description: Kindles agni and improves nutrient absorption; used as a pre-meal digestive in the classical tradition.

ingredients.name: Cumin
ingredients.description: A staple digestive spice; supports the breakdown of food and reduces ama (undigested residue).

ingredients.name: Coriander
ingredients.description: Cooling and digestive; balances the heat of other spices and supports absorption.

ingredients.name: Fennel
ingredients.description: Gently carminative; traditionally taken after meals to settle digestion and reduce bloating.

words_to_know.definition: The body's capacity to resist disease; literally 'tolerance of illness'. Discussed in the Caraka Samhita as an innate force that can be strengthened or weakened by lifestyle.
The finest, most refined product of digestion and tissue nutrition. Regarded as the biological basis of vitality, immunity, and mental calm.
Digestive fire; the transformative capacity that converts food into body tissues. Strong agni is considered essential for producing ojas.
A class of Ayurvedic treatments or herbs that rejuvenate tissues and rebuild long-term strength and immunity. Taken in courses, not as one-off remedies.
One of the seven body tissues (plasma, blood, muscle, fat, bone, marrow/nerve tissue, reproductive tissue) that are nourished in sequence through digestion.
Physical exercise as understood in Ayurveda; recommended at half one's capacity to support circulation and tissue nourishment without depleting ojas.

sources: Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana · Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana · Caraka Samhita, Chikitsasthana · Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana · Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana · Bhavaprakasha, Guduchyadi Varga
disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
